title: Impetigo
oneLiner: Superficial skin infection, mostly children, highly contagious; treat promptly to prevent spread and rare post-strep complications.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV)** — Look for punched-out erosions, pain, fever, ill appearance; rule out in any child with eczema + widespread vesicles → **immediate IV aciclovir**.
- **Severe cellulitis / sepsis** — Spreading erythema with systemic signs (tachycardia, fever, hypotension); impetigo rarely causes this, but if present, treat as sepsis.
- **Neonatal severe infection (Staph/HSV)** — Ill neonate with pustules/bullae → treat as sepsis; do blood cultures, start IV flucloxacillin + aciclovir.
sections.Presentation:
- Itchy, honey-coloured crusted lesions (non-bullous) or fragile bullae that rupture leaving a collarette of scale (bullous)
- Typically face (around mouth/nose) and limbs
- Mildly tender regional lymphadenopathy
- Often multiple lesions that coalesce
- No fever unless extensive or secondary cellulitis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Herpes simplex / eczema herpeticum** – Vesicles on erythematous base, pain, fever; look for history of eczema
- **Contact dermatitis** – Itching, not crusting, history of contactant
- **Tinea faciei / corporis** – Annular scaling, no honey crust, KOH positive
- **Bullous erythema multiforme** – Target lesions, mucosal involvement, recent HSV or drug
- **Scabies (secondarily infected)** – Widespread itch, burrows, distribution (intertriginous, wrists)

**Splitter:** Honey-coloured crusts + rapid spread in child → impetigo; painful punched-out vesicles + underlying atopic eczema → eczema herpeticum (consider urgent PCR swab)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Clinical diagnosis in typical cases; swab for culture if recurrent, treatment failure, outbreak, or suspected MRSA
- **Bloods:** Not routinely needed; check FBC/CRP if extensive or fever suggests systemic spread
- **Imaging:** Not indicated
- **Special tests:** Viral PCR swab of vesicle fluid if suspect HSV; skin scrapings for KOH if fungal doubt
- **Never-miss test:** **Swab for HSV PCR** if any vesicle + fever + widespread distribution (especially in atopic child) – delay in aciclovir can be fatal
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - For mild localised disease: prescribe topical fusidic acid 2% cream TDS × 5–7 days
  - For extensive (>3 lesions or multiple body areas) or bullous: start oral flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS × 7 days (child dose by weight [check local policy])
  - Educate: avoid touching lesions, good hand hygiene, no school/childcare until lesions crusted and dry (or 48h after starting treatment)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admission rarely needed; consider if widespread cellulitis, fever, or at-risk host (immunosuppressed, neonate)
  - IV flucloxacillin 1–2 g QDS if oral not tolerated or severe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 7-day course; review if not improved by 48–72h
  - Treat underlying eczema/excoriation; consider bleach baths for recurrent cases
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Nil specific; ensure VTE prophylaxis if prolonged stay
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line topical:** Fusidic acid 2% cream – apply TDS to affected area for 5–7 days (BNF)
- **Alternative topical:** Hydrogen peroxide 1% cream (OTC) – short course for mild non-bullous (less mupirocin resistance risk)
- **First-line oral (extensive/bullous):** Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS (adult); child dose: 12.5–25 mg/kg QDS (max 500 mg QDS) for 7 days
- **Penicillin allergy alternative:** Clarithromycin 250–500 mg BD (adult) for 7 days; child dose [check local policy]
- **MRSA suspicion:** Based on swab – [check local policy] (e.g. doxycycline, trimethoprim, clindamycin)
- **Key contraindications:** Avoid topical fusidic acid near eyes (mupirocin eye ointment preferable); avoid penicillin if immediate hypersensitivity
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None required for short courses
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing topical corticosteroids** (e.g. hydrocortisone) for impetigo – worsens infection, masks spread; also overdosing oral antibiotics (especially in children) – always weight-check
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Spread to others (outbreaks in schools/nurseries)
  - Cellulitis / lymphangitis
  - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (rare, 2–5 weeks after *Streptococcus pyogenes* impetigo) – check urine dip if presenting later with oedema/haematuria
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrence, especially with untreated eczema or minor trauma
  - Scarring (rare; mostly post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation resolves over months)
sections.Prognosis:
- Excellent with treatment – resolves in 7–10 days; no scarring or long-term issues
- Post-strep glomerulonephritis risk ~1% if untreated; treat impetigo to lower risk but does not eliminate it
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Swab before starting antibiotics** if outbreak or recurrent – avoids resistance and guides MRSA therapy
- **Check the child’s school/nursery policy** – most require 48h of treatment or until lesions are crusted before returning
- **Treat underlying eczema** – use emollients and avoid irritants to prevent recurrence
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use topical steroids** – they suppress inflammation but worsen infection
- **Do not rely on topical fusidic acid for extensive or bullous impetigo** – oral antibiotics are needed
- **Do not forget herpes in a child with eczema** – misdiagnosing eczema herpeticum as impetigo delays iv aciclovir and can be fatal
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Spreading erythema, pain, or fever develops; child becomes drowsy or unwell; lesions are not improving after 3 days of treatment
- **Follow-up:** GP in 5–7 days if not resolved; otherwise no formal follow-up needed
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / consider ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥ 5, lactate > 2 mmol/L, GCS < 15, or blistering/necrosis suggests deeper infection
- **Specific threshold:** Spreading cellulitis despite oral antibiotics – needs IV antibiotics and possible surgical review for necrotising fasciitis (rare)

rawMarkdown:
## Impetigo
**One-liner:** Superficial skin infection, mostly children, highly contagious; treat promptly to prevent spread and rare post-strep complications.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV)** — Look for punched-out erosions, pain, fever, ill appearance; rule out in any child with eczema + widespread vesicles → **immediate IV aciclovir**.
- **Severe cellulitis / sepsis** — Spreading erythema with systemic signs (tachycardia, fever, hypotension); impetigo rarely causes this, but if present, treat as sepsis.
- **Neonatal severe infection (Staph/HSV)** — Ill neonate with pustules/bullae → treat as sepsis; do blood cultures, start IV flucloxacillin + aciclovir.

### Presentation
- Itchy, honey-coloured crusted lesions (non-bullous) or fragile bullae that rupture leaving a collarette of scale (bullous)
- Typically face (around mouth/nose) and limbs
- Mildly tender regional lymphadenopathy
- Often multiple lesions that coalesce
- No fever unless extensive or secondary cellulitis

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Herpes simplex / eczema herpeticum** – Vesicles on erythematous base, pain, fever; look for history of eczema
- **Contact dermatitis** – Itching, not crusting, history of contactant
- **Tinea faciei / corporis** – Annular scaling, no honey crust, KOH positive
- **Bullous erythema multiforme** – Target lesions, mucosal involvement, recent HSV or drug
- **Scabies (secondarily infected)** – Widespread itch, burrows, distribution (intertriginous, wrists)

**Splitter:** Honey-coloured crusts + rapid spread in child → impetigo; painful punched-out vesicles + underlying atopic eczema → eczema herpeticum (consider urgent PCR swab)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Clinical diagnosis in typical cases; swab for culture if recurrent, treatment failure, outbreak, or suspected MRSA
- **Bloods:** Not routinely needed; check FBC/CRP if extensive or fever suggests systemic spread
- **Imaging:** Not indicated
- **Special tests:** Viral PCR swab of vesicle fluid if suspect HSV; skin scrapings for KOH if fungal doubt
- **Never-miss test:** **Swab for HSV PCR** if any vesicle + fever + widespread distribution (especially in atopic child) – delay in aciclovir can be fatal

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - For mild localised disease: prescribe topical fusidic acid 2% cream TDS × 5–7 days
  - For extensive (>3 lesions or multiple body areas) or bullous: start oral flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS × 7 days (child dose by weight [check local policy])
  - Educate: avoid touching lesions, good hand hygiene, no school/childcare until lesions crusted and dry (or 48h after starting treatment)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Admission rarely needed; consider if widespread cellulitis, fever, or at-risk host (immunosuppressed, neonate)
  - IV flucloxacillin 1–2 g QDS if oral not tolerated or severe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 7-day course; review if not improved by 48–72h
  - Treat underlying eczema/excoriation; consider bleach baths for recurrent cases
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Nil specific; ensure VTE prophylaxis if prolonged stay

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line topical:** Fusidic acid 2% cream – apply TDS to affected area for 5–7 days (BNF)
- **Alternative topical:** Hydrogen peroxide 1% cream (OTC) – short course for mild non-bullous (less mupirocin resistance risk)
- **First-line oral (extensive/bullous):** Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS (adult); child dose: 12.5–25 mg/kg QDS (max 500 mg QDS) for 7 days
- **Penicillin allergy alternative:** Clarithromycin 250–500 mg BD (adult) for 7 days; child dose [check local policy]
- **MRSA suspicion:** Based on swab – [check local policy] (e.g. doxycycline, trimethoprim, clindamycin)
- **Key contraindications:** Avoid topical fusidic acid near eyes (mupirocin eye ointment preferable); avoid penicillin if immediate hypersensitivity
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None required for short courses
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing topical corticosteroids** (e.g. hydrocortisone) for impetigo – worsens infection, masks spread; also overdosing oral antibiotics (especially in children) – always weight-check

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Spread to others (outbreaks in schools/nurseries)
  - Cellulitis / lymphangitis
  - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (rare, 2–5 weeks after *Streptococcus pyogenes* impetigo) – check urine dip if presenting later with oedema/haematuria
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrence, especially with untreated eczema or minor trauma
  - Scarring (rare; mostly post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation resolves over months)

### Prognosis
- Excellent with treatment – resolves in 7–10 days; no scarring or long-term issues
- Post-strep glomerulonephritis risk ~1% if untreated; treat impetigo to lower risk but does not eliminate it

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Swab before starting antibiotics** if outbreak or recurrent – avoids resistance and guides MRSA therapy
- **Check the child’s school/nursery policy** – most require 48h of treatment or until lesions are crusted before returning
- **Treat underlying eczema** – use emollients and avoid irritants to prevent recurrence

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use topical steroids** – they suppress inflammation but worsen infection
- **Do not rely on topical fusidic acid for extensive or bullous impetigo** – oral antibiotics are needed
- **Do not forget herpes in a child with eczema** – misdiagnosing eczema herpeticum as impetigo delays iv aciclovir and can be fatal

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Spreading erythema, pain, or fever develops; child becomes drowsy or unwell; lesions are not improving after 3 days of treatment
- **Follow-up:** GP in 5–7 days if not resolved; otherwise no formal follow-up needed

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / consider ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥ 5, lactate > 2 mmol/L, GCS < 15, or blistering/necrosis suggests deeper infection
- **Specific threshold:** Spreading cellulitis despite oral antibiotics – needs IV antibiotics and possible surgical review for necrotising fasciitis (rare)
